questions.question: Graph the inequality y > -3. Which description is correct?
options: Solid horizontal line at y = -3, shading above
Dashed horizontal line at y = -3, shading above
Solid horizontal line at y = -3, shading below
Dashed horizontal line at y = -3, shading below
questions.explanation: y > -3 uses a dashed line because > is strict. Shading is above the line because y-values must be greater than -3. Test point (0, 0): 0 > -3, true. (0, -3) is on the boundary and not included.

questions.question: Graph the inequality x ≤ 2. Which point is NOT in the solution region?
questions.explanation: For x ≤ 2, any point with x-coordinate greater than 2 is not a solution. (3, 1) has x = 3 > 2, so it fails. The graph uses a solid vertical line at x = 2 with shading to the left.

questions.question: Graph the inequality y ≤ 0. Which ordered pair is a solution?
questions.explanation: (2, -5) has y = -5 ≤ 0, so it satisfies the inequality. The graph is a solid horizontal line at y = 0 (the x-axis) with shading below. (3, 1) and (-1, 2) have positive y-values; (0, 4) has y = 4 > 0.

questions.question: Which inequality is represented by a dashed vertical line at x = -1 with shading to the right?
questions.explanation: A vertical line represents an x-boundary. Dashed means strict inequality (< or >). Shading to the right means x-values greater than -1, so the inequality is x > -1.

questions.question: Graph the system: x < 4 and y ≥ -2. Which point satisfies BOTH inequalities?
questions.explanation: (3, 0) satisfies both: x = 3 < 4 and y = 0 ≥ -2. (5, 1) fails x < 4. (2, -3) fails y ≥ -2. (4, -1) fails x < 4 because 4 is not strictly less than 4.

questions.question: Graph the inequality y > 5. Which point lies in the solution region?
questions.explanation: y > 5 uses a dashed horizontal line at y = 5 with shading above. (3, 6) has y = 6 > 5, so it is in the solution region. Points with y = 5 are on the dashed line and not included. Points with y < 5 fail.

questions.question: Graph the inequality x ≥ 0. Which description is correct?
options: Dashed vertical line at x = 0, shading to the right
Solid vertical line at x = 0, shading to the right
Dashed vertical line at x = 0, shading to the left
Solid vertical line at x = 0, shading to the left
questions.explanation: x ≥ 0 includes the y-axis itself (solid line) and all points to the right of it. This represents the right half-plane including the boundary.

questions.question: Which inequality is represented by a solid horizontal line at y = 1 with shading below?
questions.explanation: A solid horizontal line at y = 1 with shading below means all points with y-coordinate at or below 1 are solutions. The inequality is y ≤ 1.

questions.question: Graph the inequality x < -3. Which ordered pair is NOT a solution?
questions.explanation: x < -3 uses a dashed vertical line at x = -3. (-3, 1) has x = -3, which is not strictly less than -3, so it is not in the solution region. All other points have x < -3 and are solutions.

questions.question: Graph the system: x ≥ -2 and y < 1. Which point is in the solution region?
questions.explanation: (0, 0) satisfies both x ≥ -2 and y < 1. (-3, 0) fails the x-inequality. (0, 2) fails the y-inequality. (-1, 1) fails because y = 1 is not strictly less than 1 (dashed line, not included).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You have mastered horizontal and vertical boundary inequalities and their special cases.
scoreMessages.70: Strong skills! Focus on: 1) Horizontal boundaries (y = k) use solid lines for ≤ and ≥, dashed for < and >, 2) Vertical boundaries (x = h) follow the same rule, 3) Shading direction corresponds to the inequality sign.
scoreMessages.50: Good progress. Remember: Inequalities with only y (like y > 3) produce horizontal lines. Inequalities with only x (like x ≤ -1) produce vertical lines. Use dashed lines for strict inequalities (<, >) and solid lines for inclusive inequalities (≤, ≥).
scoreMessages.0: Start by: 1) Identify whether the inequality involves only x (vertical line), only y (horizontal line), or both, 2) Determine if the boundary is solid (≤, ≥) or dashed (<, >), 3) Shade above/below for y-inequalities, right/left for x-inequalities, 4) For systems, find the overlapping region.
